coredata.dc:title: Histidine-mediated tunable peroxidase-like activity of nanosized Pd for photometric sensing of Ag+
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenchi
Niu, Xiangheng
Meng, Suci
Li, Xin
He, Yanfang
Pan, Jianming
Qiu, Fengxian
Zhao, Hongli
Lan, Minbo
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we proposed a new and facile strategy for the high-performance colorimetric detection of Ag+ based on the tunable peroxidase-like activity of Pd nanoparticles (NPs) mediated by histidine (His). Bare Pd NPs possess intrinsic catalytic ability to trigger the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue TMBox in the presence of H2O2. After being decorated by His, the formed His-Pd nanozyme exhibits significantly improved mimetic activity due to its favorable physicochemical characteristics including smaller size, interactions between Pd and His, and better hydrophilicity, and such that an enhanced TMB color reaction is observed. When Ag+ exists, it is able to despoil the His modifier from the His-Pd surface via the specific interaction between Ag+ and His, resulting in the bald Pd again with weak activity. With this principle, highly sensitive determination of Ag+ in a linear scope of 30–300 nM was achieved with a limit of detection down to 4.7 nM. In addition, the colorimetric sensor based on the tunable enzymatic activity of nanosized Pd adjusted by His could provide excellent selectivity for Ag+ sensing against common interferents. Reliability of the strategy for practical detection of Ag+ was also demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pd-based nanozyme
Histidine
Ag+
Activity regulation
Colorimetric detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enhanced peroxidase-like activity of His-Pd compared with bare Pd
Ag+ Reduces the mimetic activity of His-Pd by despoiling His

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21605061 and 31601549 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20160489 ), the Natural Science Fund for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province (No. 16KJB150009 ), the Postdoctoral Fund of China (No. 2016M600365 ), the Postdoctoral Fund of Jiangsu Province (No. 1601015B ), the Open Fund from the Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry (No. SKLFMC201601 ), the Open Fund from the State Key Laboratory of Bioreactor Engineering , and the Cultivation Project for Excellent Young Teachers in Jiangsu University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) depicts the XRD patterns of bare Pd and His-Pd. (B) compares the FT-IR spectra of the two materials. (C) and (D) are the TEM images of bare Pd and His-Pd, respectively.

(A) presents the UV–vis spectra of the TMB-H2O2 system catalyzed by different nanozymes, and the inset shows the corresponding photographs (a―no nanozyme, b―bare Pd, and c―His-Pd). (B) exhibits the time-dependent absorbance changes of the TMB-H2O2 system under the catalysis of various peroxidase mimics.
(A) shows the high-resolution Pd 3d XPS patterns of bare Pd and His-Pd. (B) compares the hydrophilicity of the two nanozymes.

(A) depicts the UV–vis spectra of the TMB-H2O2 system catalyzed by His-Pd with the absence or presence of Ag+, and the insets are the corresponding photographs. (B) illustrates the underlying mechanism for the phenomenon observed in (A).

(A) displays the UV–vis profiles of the TMB-H2O2 system catalyzed by His-Pd with the presence of Ag+ at various concentrations, and the inset is the corresponding photograph. (B) presents the relationship between the absorbance at 652 nm and the concentration of Ag+, and the inset depicts the linear relationship.
Selective responses of the proposed method for Ag+ sensing against other common metal ions.
Comparison of the apparent kinetic parameters of His-Pd and bare Pd toward H2O2 and TMB, respectively.
Comparison of Ag+ detection performance of our sensor with previously reported methods.
Recovery results for the detection of Ag+ in environmental water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Three environmental water samples collected from different places were used.

head.ce:title: Histidine-mediated tunable peroxidase-like activity of nanosized Pd for photometric sensing of Ag+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we proposed a new and facile strategy for the high-performance colorimetric detection of Ag+ based on the tunable peroxidase-like activity of Pd nanoparticles (NPs) mediated by histidine (His). Bare Pd NPs possess intrinsic catalytic ability to trigger the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue TMBox in the presence of H2O2. After being decorated by His, the formed His-Pd nanozyme exhibits significantly improved mimetic activity due to its favorable physicochemical characteristics including smaller size, interactions between Pd and His, and better hydrophilicity, and such that an enhanced TMB color reaction is observed. When Ag+ exists, it is able to despoil the His modifier from the His-Pd surface via the specific interaction between Ag+ and His, resulting in the bald Pd again with weak activity. With this principle, highly sensitive determination of Ag+ in a linear scope of 30–300 nM was achieved with a limit of detection down to 4.7 nM. In addition, the colorimetric sensor based on the tunable enzymatic activity of nanosized Pd adjusted by His could provide excellent selectivity for Ag+ sensing against common interferents. Reliability of the strategy for practical detection of Ag+ was also demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nanosized materials with enzyme-like activity (nanozymes) have been attracting increasing interest due to their advantages of low cost, easy mass-production, good stability against denaturing, and long-term storage [1–7]. These merits endow them with promising applications in a series of fields, especially in chemo- and biosensing [8–18]. However, in comparison with natural bio-enzymes and organic catalysts, the activities of most current nanozymes are still lower, which inevitably affects their wider applications. To address this deficiency, one has developed several strategies to improve the catalytic properties of nanozymes, including controlling size [19,20], tuning morphology [21,22], screening composition [23], forming hybrids [24,25], optimizing crystal facet [26], and modifying surface [16,27–31]. Among these strategies, surface modification is especially attractive. Coatings on the surface of nanozymes are able to not only stabilize their nanosized structures but also provide potential functional groups for further bioconjugation. More importantly, appropriate surface modification can tune the catalytic activities and efficiencies of nanozymes via changing electronic structure, adjusting surface acidity, blocking surface access, promoting product desorption, and so on [31].

Up to now, a number of substances, including inorganic ions (F− [32], I− [16], Hg2+ [12,33], PO4 3- [34], S2- [35], et al.), small molecules (DNA [27,29], amino acids [36], et al.), and macromolecules (PEG [19], proteins [37], et al.), have been employed to improve the enzymatic characteristics of nanozymes. Among these modifiers, amino acids are drawing increasing attention because of their excellent biocompatibility and low cost. More importantly, using amino acids to modify the surface of nanozymes can mimic the natural bio-enzymes (in which active sites are usually surrounded by amino acids) better [36]. In addition, from the analytical chemistry point of view, amino acids are easy to interact with other species such as metal ions. Thus, selective detection of metal ions may be achieved in the amino acid-containing system.

Inspired by the above considerations, here we reported the visual sensing of Ag+ in aqueous phase based on the tunable peroxidase-like activity of nanosized Pd mediated by histidine (His). Ag and its compounds have been widely used in the electrical, photography, and pharmaceutical industries. Moreover, its compounds play an essential role in various medical applications including anti-bacteria, implanted prostheses, and water disinfection [38]. These widespread applications have resulted in the increased Ag level in environment. Besides, Ag accumulated in human body can lead to the well-known argyria. Therefore, it is of great importance to explore methods to effectively monitor the species in various environmental and biological samples. In this work, His was employed to modify the surface of Pd nanoparticles (NPs), and significantly improved activity to trigger the color reaction of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 was observed in the formed His-Pd compared with bare Pd. When Ag+ was added, it despoiled the His modifier from the His-Pd surface via the specific interaction between Ag+ and His, resulting in the bare Pd again with weak activity. With this principle, a new and facile colorimetric assay was developed for the highly sensitive and selective determination of Ag+. In comparison with conventional methods such as atomic absorption spectrometry [39], inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry [40], and voltammetry [41], the sensor developed has the unique advantage of portable monitoring with no need of sophisticate equipments, which makes it possible for in-field analysis.

PdCl2 was provided by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. NaBH4, l-glutamine (Glu), l-phenylalanine (Phe), l-tyrosine (Tyr), l-histidine (His), AgNO3, sodium acetate (NaAc), acetic acid (HAc), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and H2O2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and utilized without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the study.

The His-Pd nanozyme was synthesized using a one-pot wet-chemical method. Briefly, 5 mL of 50 mM PdCl2 solution and 10 mL of 0.1 M His solution were first mixed together with a mild stir for 1 h at room temperature; then 15 mL of 0.1 M NaBH4 solution was quickly added into the mixture for reaction for 12 h; the formed solid product was rinsed with ethanol and deionized water alternately for several times, and the material was collected by centrifugation and freeze drying for 48 h, thus the His-Pd nanozyme was obtained. To investigate the effects of different amino acids on the peroxidase-like activity of nanosized Pd, another three amino acid-decorated nanozymes (Glu-Pd, Phe-Pd, and Tyr-Pd) were also prepared using the same method. For comparison, the bare Pd counterpart was also obtained with no amino acid added into the synthetic system, as illustrated in Scheme S1 (Supporting information).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a 6100Lab diffractometer (Shimadzu) with a Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were detected by a Nicolet Nexus 470 instrument (USA Nicolet Co., Ltd.). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out on a JEM-2100F microscope (JEOL) with an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. Zeta potentials of samples in 0.2 M NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4) were performed on a Nano ZS90 instrument (UK Malvern Co., Ltd.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on an ESCALAB-MKII spectrometer (Thermo-Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd.) with an Al Kα radiation as the excitation source. Water contact angles of samples were obtained at room temperature using a KSV CM200 instrument (Finland Attention Co., Ltd.) combined with a high speed camera.

The peroxidase-like activities of the synthesized materials were investigated by the catalytic oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. All the reactions were monitored by a Cary 8454 ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrometer (Agilent Technologies Co., Ltd.). A 5 mM TMB stock solution was prepared with ethanol for use. H2O2 stock solutions with various concentrations were daily prepared. 0.2 M NaAc-HAc solutions with different pH values (adjusted by diluted HCl or NaOH) were prepared as the incubation buffer. Typically, the colorimetric measurements were performed in a 5 mL cylinder with 3 mL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0) consisting of 5.55 μg/mL nanozyme, 0.327 M H2O2, and 0.167 mM TMB. The time-dependent absorbance changes were recorded with a 30 s interval. The steady-state kinetic measurements were carried out by recording the absorbance at 652 nm at a 5 s interval within 1 min. The apparent kinetic parameters were calculated based on the equation ʋ = V max × [S]/(K m + [S]), where ʋ is the initial velocity, V max is the maximum reaction velocity, [S] is the substrate (H2O2 or TMB) concentration, and K m is the Michaelis-Menten constant.

For the detection of Ag+, the target with various concentrations was mixed with the His-Pd peroxidase mimic in 0.2 M NaAc-HAc solution (pH 4.0) for 10 min. Then, 50 μL of 9.8 M H2O2 and 50 μL of 5 mM TMB were added into the mixture. After reaction for 15 min, the solution was ready for colorimetric measurements.

In the present study, the His-Pd material was prepared by a commonly used reduction approach in aqueous phase at room temperature. As illustrated in Scheme S1 (Supporting information), PdCl2 and NaBH4 are employed as the Pd precursor and the reductant, respectively. His is added into the synthetic system to obtain the His-Pd nanozyme. For comparison, the bare Pd counterpart was also synthesized under identical conditions with no His added.

First, the synthesized materials were characterized by several means. Fig. 1 (A) compares the XRS patterns of His-Pd and bare Pd. The bare Pd provides three remarkable diffraction signals at 2θ = 40.12°, 46.66° and 68.12°, which should be attributed to the (111), (200) and (220) planes of the face-centered cubic structure of Pd (JCPDS No. 46-1043). These peaks are well defined with narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) and high intensity, indicative of a good crystallinity of the bare Pd. With regard to His-Pd, three recognizable peaks of Pd located at the same places are also observed. Due to the modification of His on the Pd surface, these diffraction signals turn to be less remarkable, providing wider FWHM and lower intensity. In the FT-IR spectra (B), the bare Pd exhibits no unique absorption signal. In comparison with bare Pd, the His-Pd material provides several notable peaks: the significantly enhanced broad peak in the wavenumber window of 3150–3700 cm−1 is ascribed to the −OH group and the NH2 group in His, the CH stretching vibration offers the absorption peaks at around 2900 cm−1, the increased signal at ～1650 cm−1 is due to the −COOH group in His, and in the 800–1400 cm−1 wavenumber range the His-Pd mimic also provides corresponding absorption signals. The FT-IR result clearly demonstrates that His has been successfully modified onto the Pd surface via the one-pot wet-chemical method.

Furthermore, the morphology of the synthesized His-Pd was observed by TEM. As shown in (C), irregular particles with a large size (＞10 nm) are obtained for bare Pd. The His-Pd material also exhibits a shape of NPs (D), while these particles have a smaller size (～4.4 nm). Both Pd and His-Pd NPs show a certain agglomeration, while no precipitate of these particles is found in aqueous solution with aging time. Zeta potential measurements indicate that, in comparison with the bare Pd (with a Zeta potential of 10.2 mV), the modification of His onto the Pd surface (with a Zeta potential of 11.5 mV) increases its dispersibility in the NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced peroxidase-like activity of His-Pd compared with bare Pd
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the potential peroxidase-like properties of the synthesized Pd-His were investigated using TMB and H2O2 as the two substrates. As depicted in Fig. 2 (A), in the absence of nanozymes, the TMB-H2O2 system provides no notable UV–vis absorption. When the synthesized His-Pd is added into the system, a remarkable color reaction occurs, providing a maximum absorbance at ～652 nm. This phenomenon is ascribed to the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of His-Pd. The material acting as a mimic can catalyze the decomposition of the H2O2 substrate into hydroxyl radicals, which further oxidize the colorless TMB substrate into its blue product TMBox [42]. Similar to natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the peroxidase-like activity of His-Pd is also affected by reaction conditions (temperature, pH, etc.). As shown in Fig. S1(A) (Supporting information), a volcano-type relationship between the His-Pd activity and the buffer pH is observed, along with a maximum activity at pH 4. As for the influence of reaction temperature, the mimetic activity of His-Pd decreases with the increasing reaction temperature (B). Thus, an optimal pH 4 and a reaction temperature of 25 °C were used in the following study.

For comparison, the catalytic features of bare Pd were also studied under identical conditions. When bare Pd is used as a peroxidase mimic instead of His-Pd, the TMB color reaction also occurs, while the UV–vis absorbance signal, as presented in Fig. 2(A), turns to be much smaller in comparison with that observed in His-Pd. This comparison suggests that the decoration of His on Pd can significantly improve its mimetic activity. The higher activity of His-Pd compared with bare Pd is also demonstrated by the time-dependent absorbance curves (B). Obviously, His-Pd can induce the color reaction much faster than bare Pd. In this research, another three amino acids (Tyr, Phe, and Glu) were also modified onto the Pd surface to further investigate their effects on the peroxidase-like activity of nanosized Pd. As presented in Fig. S2(A) (Supporting information), the four amino acids have different molecular structures with various physicochemical features, and they may bring some interesting influences on the Pd-based nanozyme. As a result, Tyr and Phe as well as His are found to have the capacity to promote the TMB color reaction, while Glu-Pd shows equal mimetic activity to bare Pd (B). Among these modified Pd-based nanozymes, the proposed His-Pd shows the highest mimetic activity to catalyze the TMB-H2O2 system. This phenomenon is in agreement with that observed in Fe3O4 NPs [36]. The result indicates that different amino acids modified onto the Pd core affects its peroxidase-like properties at various degrees.

To further evaluate the catalytic activity of His-Pd in contrast to that of bare Pd, steady-state kinetic measurements for the two nanozymes were carried out. As depicted in Fig. S3 (Supporting information), typical Michaelis-Menten curves are observed for both bare Pd and His-Pd. The apparent kinetic parameters are listed in Table 1 . The obtained K m values in His-Pd are smaller than those in bare Pd, suggesting that the modification of His onto Pd can improve the affinities of the two substrates to the nanozyme. The k cat values of His-Pd toward the H2O2 and TMB substrates are ～1.2 times larger than those of the bare Pd counterpart. These data also confirm that the His-Pd is a desirable peroxidase mimic with improved activity toward both TMB and H2O2.

The question why the synthesized His-Pd can offer promoted enzymatic activity and efficiency compared with bare Pd has inspired us to explore the underlying reasons. Firstly, as compared in Fig. 1(C) and (D), the presence of the His amino acid in the synthetic system leads to a smaller size of Pd NPs. It is well known that the size of nanozymes affects their enzymatic activities dramatically [19]. The smaller particles have a greater surface-to-volume ratio to interact with substrates. Secondly, since the catalytic activities of nanozymes greatly depend on their surface chemistry, the decoration of His on Pd may make some impacts on its surface features. Fig. 3 (A) compares the Pd 3d XPS patterns of His-Pd and bare Pd. As observed, the corresponding peaks in His-Pd shift to lower binding energies in comparison with the bare Pd counterpart. These shifts are ascribed to the interactions between His and Pd, which further affect the electronic structure of Pd. In addition, the proportion of Pd° in His-Pd is calculated to be 66.5%, higher than that of the bare Pd (56.3%). This means that the decoration of His on the Pd surface increases the Pd° percentage. According to the peroxidase mechanism for noble metal (M) nanozymes, metallic M° participates in the activation of the H2O2 substrate to release hydroxyl radicals [33,43]. Thus, the increase of the Pd° percentage in His-Pd will rationally improve its activity and efficiency. Thirdly, in comparison with bare Pd (providing a contact angel of 127.8°), His-Pd (with a contact angel of 27.9°) is more hydrophilic (B), which is also beneficial for the interactions between the nanozyme and the two substrates. Taken together, the better catalytic activity and efficiency observed in His-Pd result from the synergy of its favorable physicochemical characteristics, including smaller size, interactions between Pd and His, and better hydrophilicity.

In contrast to natural enzymes, one of the most attractive merits of nanozymes is their stronger resistance against harsh environments. To check this feature, we first incubated the His-Pd nanozyme in solutions with various pH values or at different temperatures for 2 h, and then measured its activity under standard conditions. Our previous studies [15,18] have revealed that natural HRP exhibits high activity only in neutral media. With pH decreases or increases, its activity is sharply reduced, and no activity is found in strong acid solutions. With regard to temperature, the activity of HRP rapidly decreases when the incubation temperature exceeds 45 °C. In contrast, as depicted in Fig. S4 (Supporting information), the synthesized His-Pd, similar to bare Pd, exhibits no remarkable change in activity when it is incubated in buffers with a wide range of pH. Its activity also has no notable change upon temperature. These results verify the excellent robustness of the His-Pd peroxidase mimic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ag+ Reduces the mimetic activity of His-Pd by despoiling His
ce:section.ce:para: With no doubt, the excellent enzymatic activity and robustness of the proposed His-Pd will bring it to wide and promising applications. In the present study, we used it to sense Ag+ in aqueous phase. It is interestingly found that the presence of a trace amount of Ag+ in the TMB-H2O2 system catalyzed by His-Pd can suppress the color reaction. As shown in Fig. 4 (A), with the absence of Ag+, the His-Pd nanozyme can efficiently catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2, providing a remarkable UV–vis adsorption peak at 652 nm. When 500 nM Ag+ is added into the system, a significantly reduced color change is observed. It is further found that when bare Pd is used as the peroxidase-like nanozyme, the addition of Ag+ hardly results in the obvious change of the color reaction (Fig. S5, Supporting information). This phenomenon should be attributed to the change of the nanozyme activity mediated by His and Ag+. The previous study [14] has reported that the Ag+ species could selectively bind to Pd° through metallophilic interactions and induce an apparent aggregation of Pd NPs. In our work, both the as-synthesized bare Pd and His-Pd NPs exhibit a certain agglomeration, as verified by the TEM images shown in Fig. 1(C) and (D). These agglomerated nanoparticles still exhibit different peroxidase-mimicking activities, as demonstrated by Fig. 2. This reasonably excludes the explanation that the reduced peroxidase-like activity of His-Pd NPs results from their possible agglomeration induced by the Ag+ species added. As a matter of fact, Ag+ is prone to combine the His species to form the His-Ag+ complex (Scheme S2, Supporting information) [44–46]. Therefore, as illustrated in (B), the synthesized His-Pd has high enzymatic activity to catalyze the TMB-H2O2 system, resulting in an enhanced TMB color reaction. When Ag+ is added, the Ag+ species despoils the His modifier from the Pd surface via the specific interaction between Ag+ and His (Scheme S2, Supporting information), thus resulting in the bald Pd again with weak activity to induce the color reaction.

Based on the above His-mediated tunable peroxidase-like activity of nanosized Pd, a facile and high-performance colorimetric chemosensor was fabricated for Ag+ detection. Fig. 5 (A) displays the UV–vis profiles of the TMB-H2O2 system catalyzed by His-Pd with the presence of Ag+ at various concentrations. Along with the increasing Ag+ level, the UV–vis adsorption signal decreases gradually. Fig. 5(B) records the relationship between the absorbance at 652 nm and the concentration of Ag+. A linear curve of the absorbance upon the Ag+ content in a range of 30–300 nM is obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) is down to 4.7 nM calculated based on the signal-to-noise of three (S/N = 3) rule. We further compared the fabricated sensor with previously reported methods for Ag+ sensing. As listed Table 2 , our assay is able to offer comparable or better performance in terms of linear range and LOD. More attractively, the target can be visually detected with naked eyes by using our sensor, and no other equipment is required. The sensor can also be equipped with a smartphone for in-field analysis [47]. Besides, the colorimetric sensor has advantages of portability, low cost, and short measurement time. These advantages will endow it with great promise for on-site and real time detection applications.

To evaluate the selectivity of the developed Ag+ sensor, possible interferences from a number of common metal ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Au3+, and Hg2+) were studied. As depicted in Fig. 6 , these species with the same concentration as Ag+ affect little on the specific sensing of the analyte. This result verifies the selectivity of the Ag+ sensor. The good selectivity is closely related to the binding capacities of these species with His. As shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting information), His contains a unique imidazole functional group. For amino acids that have N-bearing side-chains like His, their relative Ag+ binding energies are larger than other ion binding energies [46]. As depicted in Scheme S2 (Supporting information), the His species can selectively coordinate Ag+ through the N atoms from the amino group and imidazole ring as well as the O atom from the carboxylate group [46].

To further demonstrate the practicability of our colorimetric sensor for detecting Ag+, three environmental water samples collected from different places were tested. Prior to measurements, the water samples were filtrated through a 0.45 μm membrane to remove any solid residual. As summarized in Table 3 , the fabricated sensor provides satisfactory recoveries for the determination of the target in real samples, indicative of its great promise for practical applications.

In summary, we have reported the significantly “enhanced” and “reduced” peroxidase-like activities of nanosized Pd mediated by His and Ag+, respectively. A new Ag+ colorimetric chemosensor based on the principle has been developed. The sensor can detect the target with wide linear range, low LOD, and good selectivity against common metal ions, and it can also be employed to detect Ag+ reliably in environmental water samples. With the merits of simple fabrication, high analytical performance, and visual readout with naked eyes, our proposed strategy based on the His-mediated tunable activity of Pd shows great promise for analytical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21605061 and 31601549), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20160489), the Natural Science Fund for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province (No. 16KJB150009), the Postdoctoral Fund of China (No. 2016M600365), the Postdoctoral Fund of Jiangsu Province (No. 1601015B), the Open Fund from the Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry (No. SKLFMC201601), the Open Fund from the State Key Laboratory of Bioreactor Engineering, and the Cultivation Project for Excellent Young Teachers in Jiangsu University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.06.071.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wenchi Zhang received her BS degree in applied chemistry from Jiangsu University in 2016, and now is a master candidate in the same university. Her research interests focus on the development of new nanozymes and their applications in colorimetric analysis.

Xiangheng Niu received his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry in 2015 from East China University of Science and Technology, and now works as an associate research fellow in Jiangsu University. He has published more than 50 peer-review scientific papers on electroanalysis, electrochemical (bio)sensors and nanozymes. His current interests include electrochemical (bio)sensors and nanozymes.
Suci Meng now is an associate professor in Jiangsu University, and her current research activities are in the area of chemical simulation and calculation.

Xin Li received her BS degree in applied chemistry from Jiangsu University in 2015, and now is a doctor candidate in the same university. Her current research activities are in the area of nanozymes with a focus on their applications in photoanalysis and electroanalysis.

Yanfang He received her BS degree in applied chemistry from Jiangsu University in 2015, and now is a master candidate in the same university. Her research interests also focus on the development of new nanozymes and their applications in colorimetric analysis.

Jianming Pan received his Ph.D. degree in environmental engineering in 2012 from Jiangsu University, and now works as a professor of applied chemistry in the same university. His research interests include functional polymers and their applications in selective capture and separation, and photo/electroanalysis. He has published over 70 peer-review research papers and patents.

Fengxian Qiu is a professor of polymer physics and chemistry in Jiangsu University. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in 2005 from Southeast University. She has published over 100 peer-review research papers and patents. Her research interests include polymer chemistry, photocatalysis and photoanalysis.

Hongli Zhao received her Ph.D. in pharmaceutical engineering and technology in 2007 from East China University of Science and Technology (ECUST), and is now a professor in ECUST. Her research focuses on electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and electrochemical biosensors.

Minbo Lan is a professor of applied chemistry in East China University of Science and Technology (ECUST). He received his PhD in applied chemistry in 1993 from ECUST. His research interests include organic functional materials and the application of nanomaterials in electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and electrochemical biosensors. He has published over 100 research papers and patents and received many awards. He has held visiting fellowships in Japan and Belgium, and is the Chief Scientist of the Shanghai Nanotechnology Promotion Centre.
